WaPageTitle.value: Phyllida Lloyd & Whitney White - Rolex mentoring programme

metadescription.value: The mentorship between Phyllida Lloyd and her protégée Whitney White enabled rich discussions about the shape of future theatre. More on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: A symbiotic journey - Rolex mentoring programme

text.value: Drawn together by a mutual love of Shakespeare, Phyllida Lloyd and her protégée Whitney White found that their relationship quickly grew, developing deeper synergies as they explored the influences shaping today’s stage.

“In many ways, if ever there was a moment for a young artist to have a mentor it’s now,” says Phyllida Lloyd thoughtfully. “The effect of Covid was to leave young artists marooned in a room, with all their projects grinding to a halt just at the moment when you are trying to work out who you are and what your place is in the culture.”

Her protégée, the director, actor and musician Whitney White, nods her head, agreeing fervently. “We were all very vulnerable. Theatre is a discipline of gathering, so this disease has been quite a challenge. Being in the Rolex mentoring programme at such a time of crisis allowed us to short cut some of the pleasantries and just say: ‘I really need your help with this. What do you think?’

“I see Phyllida as a battle buddy. It feels like she is right next to you and you are going through a journey together.”

The process of asking for advice quickly became a two-way process between the London-based director Lloyd and Chicago-born, New York-based White. In the aftermath of the Black Lives Matter protests and the discussions about the shape of future theatre they prompted, Lloyd found herself engaged in deep conversations with her protégée about her responsibilities and whether it was right for her to direct a show such as TINA: The Tina Turner Musical about the life of Tina Turner.

“I have to commend Rolex not just for bringing us together, but for enabling conversations between all the mentors, having these debates with Spike Lee, Carrie Mae Weems, Lin-Manuel Miranda, about our responsibilities,” Lloyd says. “I had detailed talks with Whitney too. It means something to artists of her generation to see a woman doing the kind of work she would like to do.”

Once again, White totally concurs. “There is such a thing as expertise, aesthetic excellence and collaboration. I do believe that as much as Phyllida can direct TINA, I can direct Ibsen. The move to shift to more equitable representation is important because we are letting in people who haven’t been able to sit at the table. I want people to have the right to tell their own stories. But we can’t be siloed by identity politics.”

White becomes emotional when she talks about the ways Lloyd, famous as director of shows as varied as an all-woman Shakespeare trilogy and the worldwide hit musical Mamma Mia, has inspired her. “When you look at this incredible career she has had, she doesn’t say ‘no’ to herself. She does Mamma Mia in the theatre, then she does Iron Lady on film. It’s the way she powerfully and quietly takes up these different spaces that is quite profound to me.”

title.value: She is being deluged with opportunities, so my main use is in giving her the courage to say ‘no’.

text.value:
The two were drawn to each other by a shared love of Shakespeare and of music, and by an interest in telling women’s stories; as their relationship has grown, they have found deeper synergies. Lloyd has helped White to develop a project that focuses on Emilia, Iago’s wife in Shakespeare’s Othello and is encouraging her to get it staged in Europe.

On a visit to London, Lloyd also introduced White to British theatre-makers. “We had an absolute roller coaster of a week seeing shows, going to galleries,” she says. “Whitney played the piano for me in my kitchen – and it was full on, experiencing the city.”

“I feel when I am speaking to her, we are speaking the same language,” says White. “But Phyllida is a phenomenon to me. She has such calm and peace around her, an ability to maintain power in the room without a chokehold, an ability to hear and listen. She is gentle with herself and others and leads a good life. I didn’t think that was possible, because being a freelance director is so mad and insane. Meeting her has made me feel very motivated – to realize that it is possible for a woman to be a multifaceted director in her own way.”

title.value: I see Phyllida as a battle buddy. It feels like she is right next to you and you are going through a journey together.

text.value:
White’s own life, from proud daughter of a supportive single mother to musician and director, has taken in acting in musicals and theatre, writing and filmmaking. She received her Master’s from Brown University in the U.S. and spent time at the prestigious Institut d’études politiques de Paris studying politics. Her constant aim has been to avoid being typecast and to allow her rich interests to flourish. Now her career is flying. Her production of Our Dear Dead Drug Lord by Alexis Scheer won an Obie Award; she is in much demand.

“She doesn’t really need me as a mentor,” says Lloyd with a laugh. “She is being deluged with opportunities, so my main use is in giving her the courage to say ‘no’. But what we do share is a belief that who you are in your life, how you conduct yourself, cannot be separated from the work that you do. Not everyone would concur with that, but we both think that is quite important.”
